item.title: French dream turns to reality
item.description: FOR most young rugby players, earning an overseas contract is the fulfilment of a dream. But for 17-year-old Jeremiah James Balemaivatubalavu Suveinaika, that dream has already become a reality.
item.content:encoded:
FOR most young rugby players, earning an overseas contract is the fulfilment of a dream.

But for 17-year-old Jeremiah James Balemaivatubalavu Suveinaika, that dream has already become a reality.

The Year 12 student at Suva Grammar School recently secured a rugby scholarship with French club Lyon, a significant milestone for the talented back who hails from Wailotua, Wainibuka, in Tailevu.

As the eldest of five siblings, Suveinaika’s rugby journey has been built on hard work, sacrifice and commitment to continuous improvement.

Comfortable at both second five-eighth and fullback, he has established himself as one of the promising young talents in secondary schools’ rugby and is now preparing to take the next step in his development overseas.

Initially, Suveinaika had secured a rugby scholarship to attend Hamilton Boys’ High School in New Zealand next year.

However, an opportunity to further his rugby development in France emerged after Flying Fijians stars Iosefo Masi and Jiuta Wainiqolo approached and encouraged him to pursue a scholarship with Lyon.

“After New Year, they messaged me. They (Masi and Wainiqolo) have been contacting me, and they said this is a good opportunity for me. I feel very lucky because I got this contract at a young age,” he said.

Despite the excitement surrounding his move to France, Suveinaika remains grounded and focused on the task at hand.

Inspired by players such as Damian McKenzie and Jerome Kaino, he hopes the opportunity at Lyon will help elevate his game and expose him to a higher standard of rugby.

“Damian McKenzie is one player I always look up to. He’s not the biggest player on the field, but every time he plays, he gives everything for his team,” Suveinaika said.

“I’ve been watching him since I was young, and I try to learn from the way he plays and carries himself on the field.”

He also credits former All Blacks loose forward Jerome Kaino as a major influence.

“I used to watch Jerome Kaino when I was growing up. His work ethic and toughness inspired me.”

Suveinaika believes the scholarship will provide valuable opportunities to learn from the experienced coaches and compete against some of the world’s best young players.

“I’m still only 17, and I know there’s a lot for me to learn. There are many good players in France, and I believe the coaches there can help me improve and become a better rugby player in the future.”

While France represents an exciting chapter ahead, his immediate focus remains on Suva Grammar School and the upcoming Deans’ competition.

Suveinaika is part of a talented under-17 side that has enjoyed remarkable success over the past two seasons.

Since winning the under-15 Deans title in 2024, the group has remained unbeaten, adding the under-16 championship last year to establish itself as one of the most dominant age-grade teams in the competition.

He played a key role in last year’s campaign, helping Grammar defeat Gospel High School in both the Southern Zone final and the national final.

Now, the team is chasing a third consecutive title.

Suva Grammar’s under-17 side will open its Southern Zone campaign against Jai Narayan College this Saturday, a match Suveinaika expects to be a stern test despite his team’s impressive record.

“We don’t underestimate any team. Every game is a final because we know this year will be tough for us.”

Before beginning his overseas journey, Suveinaika has one goal in mind: helping Suva Grammar continue its winning legacy and bring another Deans title back to Veiuto.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Caine targets badminton revival in the West
item.description: BADMINTON Fiji president Abel Caine is determined to revive badminton in the Western Division, with plans to establish more training facilities and increase participation among schools and clubs. Speaking on the future of the sport in the West, Kane highlighted the lack of badminton courts in Lautoka as one of the biggest challenges facing aspiring players.
item.content:encoded:
BADMINTON Fiji president Abel Caine is determined to revive badminton in the Western Division, with plans to establish more training facilities and increase participation among schools and clubs.

Speaking on the future of the sport in the West, Kane highlighted the lack of badminton courts in Lautoka as one of the biggest challenges facing aspiring players.

“The question arises, where will all these Lautoka players train? Where are the badminton courts in Lautoka? Unfortunately, today, there are none,” Caine said.

He said the situation was in stark contrast to developments in Nadi, where Badminton Fiji recently signed a two-year partnership agreement with the Fiji Muslim League.

Under the agreement, four badminton courts will be established at Nadi Muslim College, providing a training base for students from nearby schools, including Nadi Muslim Primary School.

Caine said his next goal is to work with Tilak High School to develop badminton facilities in Lautoka.

“We are going to Tilak High School and try to convince them to enter into a partnership agreement with Badminton Fiji. If we can improve the surface, paint the markings for two badminton courts and provide equipment, they should form a permanent Tilak team and allow other schools and the Lautoka Badminton Club to use the facilities.”

He said there was strong demand from badminton players in Lautoka, but the absence of courts had limited opportunities for growth.

Caine recalled that badminton once thrived in Lautoka, particularly through Natabua High School.

“From 2009 to 2018, Natabua High School was the dominant school champion in Fiji badminton. From one court, they produced many Fiji representatives and Pacific Games gold medalists.”

However, he said the sport declined after the school stopped playing badminton and later had to use its hall as additional classroom space.

“They’ve run out of classrooms, so they can no longer use the hall for badminton. When badminton stopped at Natabua, badminton in Lautoka died.”

Despite the challenges, Kane remains optimistic that new facilities in Nadi and Lautoka will help rebuild the sport and strengthen Western representation at national tournaments.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Blues backed by Fijian rugby league talent
item.description: FIJIAN flair will once again light up rugby league’s biggest representative stage when players of Fijian heritage feature in Game Two of the State of Origin series next Wednesday. The strong Fijian presence in the New South Wales Blues squad continues to highlight the growing influence of Fiji and the Pacific Islands in the National Rugby League competition.
item.content:encoded:
FIJIAN flair will once again light up rugby league’s biggest representative stage when players of Fijian heritage feature in Game Two of the State of Origin series next Wednesday.

The strong Fijian presence in the New South Wales Blues squad continues to highlight the growing influence of Fiji and the Pacific Islands in the National Rugby League competition.

Leading the Fijian contingent is Vodafone Fiji Bati hooker Apisai Koroisau, whose experience and leadership will be vital as the Blues look to strengthen their grip on the series.

Joining Koroisau in the squad is Mark Nawaqanitawase, who has been named on the extended bench following his impressive performances this season.

Blues coach Laurie Daley confirmed his 21-man squad for the clash at the Melbourne Cricket Ground, with Koroisau coming into the side following the injury to Blayke Brailey.

The duo’s selection is further evidence of the significant contribution players of Fijian heritage continue to make at the highest level of rugby league.

Their achievements also serve as inspiration for young players throughout Fiji and the Pacific who dream of reaching the sport’s elite level.

State of Origin remains one of rugby league’s toughest and most prestigious contests, and the continued presence of Fijian-heritage players on this stage showcases the depth of talent emerging from the Pacific.

New South Wales holds a 1-0 lead in the series after producing the biggest comeback in Origin history to defeat Queensland 22-20 in Sydney on Round One.

The Blues will face the Maroons in Game Two at the MCG next Wednesday at 10.05pm.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: FACT ticket sales begin
item.description: FOOTBALL fans across Fiji are being urged to prepare as pre-sale tickets for the BiC Fiji FACT 2026 officially opened yesterday, giving supporters an early chance to secure seats for one of the country’s biggest football tournaments. Tickets are available online through ticketmate.com.fj, while fans who cannot attend matches in person will also have the option to purchase Pay-Per-View (PPV) access
item.content:encoded:
FOOTBALL fans across Fiji are being urged to prepare as pre-sale tickets for the BiC Fiji FACT 2026 officially opened yesterday, giving supporters an early chance to secure seats for one of the country’s biggest football tournaments.

Tickets are available online through ticketmate.com.fj, while fans who cannot attend matches in person will also have the option to purchase Pay-Per-View (PPV) access via maitvfiji.com, allowing them to watch every match live from anywhere in the world.

The tournament will be staged at Subrail Park in Labasa, with group stage matches set for June 19-21. The semi-finals will follow on June 27 and grand final on June 28.

According to the Fiji Football Association chief executive officer Mohammed Yusuf, excitement is already building nationwide following several key player movements during the mid-season transfer window.

“The BiC Fiji FACT is the first major tournament on the Fiji FA calendar, and we are expecting a very exciting competition this year.

“Several teams have secured major signings during the transfer window, which has added another level of interest and competitiveness to the tournament.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Heavyweight U18 clashes set to ignite
item.description: THE battle for supremacy in the West Secondary Schools Rugby Union competition is expected to intensify this Saturday as teams return for the second round of matches across the region. Several exciting encounters are on the cards in the Under-18 grade, with Ba Methodist High School taking on Tilak High School, while Ra High School faces Ba Provincial Freebird Institute. In Nadi, rugby fans can loo
item.content:encoded:
THE battle for supremacy in the West Secondary Schools Rugby Union competition is expected to intensify this Saturday as teams return for the second round of matches across the region.

Several exciting encounters are on the cards in the Under-18 grade, with Ba Methodist High School taking on Tilak High School, while Ra High School faces Ba Provincial Freebird Institute. In Nadi, rugby fans can look forward to another blockbuster clash when Ratu Navula College hosts Natabua High School.

With competition points becoming increasingly valuable, teams will be eager to build momentum and strengthen their positions on the ladder.

Ba Methodist High School will be one of the sides to watch this season. Boosted by the presence of several players from the Ba Rugby Academy, the side has shown promising signs early in the campaign.

Their opening-round performance highlighted their potential after they battled to a hard-fought 14-14 draw against a strong Ra High School outfit last week.

That result has set the stage for what promises to be another demanding challenge against Tilak High School, who will be determined to make a statement of their own.

As the race towards the knockout stages gathers pace, supporters can expect fast-paced rugby, fierce contests and plenty of school pride on display throughout the Western Division this weekend.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Nakacia to start for Drua
item.description: THE McDonald’s Fijian Drua Women have made several key changes to their lineup ahead of their first home match of the 2026 Super Rugby Women’s season against the Western Force at Churchill Park in Lautoka on Saturday. Head coach Mike Legge has handed exciting outside centre Vika Nakacia her official Drua debut, with the former Fiji Airways Fijiana 7s representative earning a place in the starting
item.content:encoded:
THE McDonald’s Fijian Drua Women have made several key changes to their lineup ahead of their first home match of the 2026 Super Rugby Women’s season against the Western Force at Churchill Park in Lautoka on Saturday.

Head coach Mike Legge has handed exciting outside centre Vika Nakacia her official Drua debut, with the former Fiji Airways Fijiana 7s representative earning a place in the starting side.

Originally from Matacawalevu in Yasawa with maternal links to Navotua, Nakacia brings speed and experience to the backline. The former Ba Provincial Free Bird Institute student has also represented the Yasawa Women’s Rugby team and will be eager to make an impact in front of home fans.

Legge has also reshuffled his forward pack following last week’s outing. Powerful forward Mereoni Nakesa shifts to the No.8 jersey, while Tropik Wood Top Tackler Alfreda Fisher moves to openside flanker. Kolora Lomani has been named at halfback.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Raluve pathway shapes champions
item.description: THE Weet-Bix Raluve Competition, Fiji’s premier rugby tournament for girls, was successfully launched yesterday at the Suva Grammar School Hall in Nasese. This year, the competition is set to grow even bigger and better, with more schools confirmed to participate.
item.content:encoded:
THE Weet-Bix Raluve Competition, Fiji’s premier rugby tournament for girls, was successfully launched yesterday at the Suva Grammar School Hall in Nasese.

This year, the competition is set to grow even bigger and better, with more schools confirmed to participate.

Chief guest Koli Sewabu, CEO of Fiji Rugby Union, described the competition as the cornerstone of women’s rugby in Fiji, unveiling new female rugby talents through fierce contests fuelled by school pride, rivalry, and passion.

“This is where dreams take their first run, and this is where the future of Fijian women’s rugby is shaped, score by score, player by player,” he said.

“For us, this competition means more than school pride and trophies; it is a vital pathway for young women like you to earn a spot in elite teams that represent Fiji on the world stage.”

He added that rugby has also become a sustainable career, whether as a player, official, or in a supportive role.

“Our current Fijiana, who also play for the Drua in Super W, are full-time contracted rugby players. Rugby has become a career that can uplift you, your family, and your community.”

Major sponsors CJ’s Patel Group, through Sanitarium Weet-Bix and Rewa Life, reaffirmed their ongoing four-year commitment to supporting grassroots rugby.

Group Marketing Manager Monisha Prasad highlighted the positive impact the tournament has had on young girls, creating strong role models.

“Rugby is more than just a sport in Fiji; it is a powerful platform for learning essential skills such as teamwork, resilience, and discipline. It fosters friendships and community spirit,” she asserted.

“For young athletes, it offers the chance to dream big for themselves and discover how far their dedication can take them, both on and off the field.”

The first rounds of the Raluve zone competitions began last week, with round two set to kick off this Saturday.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Supermarket partners with snooker meet
item.description: Retail grocery and wholesale supermarket chain Shop N Save has come on board to lend a timely boost as sponsors of the East v West snooker and billiard competition in Suva this Sunday. The supermarket extends a long-standing partnership with the competition, which returns to Suva after it was held in Lautoka last year.
item.content:encoded:
Retail grocery and wholesale supermarket chain Shop N Save has come on board to lend a timely boost as sponsors of the East v West snooker and billiard competition in Suva this Sunday.

The supermarket extends a long-standing partnership with the competition, which returns to Suva after it was held in Lautoka last year.

President of the hosts, the Southern Division Billiard and Snooker Association, David Shiu Ratnam thanked the supermarket for their continued backing of the tournament.

According to him, the supermarket has covered expenses relating to the transportation of players, food and uniforms.

These are expenses for players travelling from the West and others in the East.

“I thank Shop N Save for coming on board for snooker and supporting snooker every year and they’ve covered the travelling expenses for both sides, the uniforms and the food,” Ratnam explained.

Shop N Save’s backing has added a timely boost for the tournament, where new players are stepping in to compete and the seasoned ones continue to show their brilliance on the billiard table.

“There are many young players which we have got playing this year.”

The competition, sponsored by supermarket chain Shop N Save, will be staged for 80 players across a variety of categories as Team West will defend the title.

Team West won 10 out of 17 frames against Team East.

Players will compete in nine singles events, six doubles, a veteran clash and a billiard clash.

The event will be held at the Merchant Club on Butt St opposite Pacific House.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
